title: Motivation theories
summary: 9609 AS — Taylor, Mayo, Maslow, Herzberg, and applying theories to case firms.
learningObjectives: Explain Taylor, Mayo, Maslow, and Herzberg theories.
Compare scientific management vs human relations approaches.
Apply theories to analyse case motivation problems.
Evaluate strengths and limitations of each theory.

simpleExplanation.title: Why people work hard
simpleExplanation.summary: Motivation theories explain what drives effort — from money and control (Taylor) to social belonging (Mayo) to fulfilling higher needs (Maslow, Herzberg).
steps: Taylor: piece rate, scientific management, money primary.
Mayo: social factors, teamwork, being consulted.
Maslow: hierarchy of needs — five levels.

flashcards.back: Workers motivated mainly by money; piece rate; management designs optimal tasks.
Social interaction and being valued improve output — not just pay.
Physiological, safety, social, esteem, self-actualisation — lower before higher.
Achievement, recognition, work itself, responsibility, advancement — true motivators.
Pay, conditions, supervision, company policy — prevent dissatisfaction only.
Treats workers as machines; ignores social and higher needs; deskilling.
Simple repetitive tasks, clear output measure — e.g. piece-rate manufacturing.
Name theory → explain → apply to case → evaluate limitation.

sections.content: **Motivation theories** (2.2.3) is core **9609 AS** content. Examiners expect **named theorists** applied to the **case** — not textbook paragraphs without context.

items: **Taylor:** Financial incentives, time-and-motion studies; suits routine manual work.
**Mayo:** Teamwork, communication, lighting/social experiments; human relations school.
**Maslow:** Unmet lower needs block higher motivation — tailor policy to workforce.
**Herzberg:** Improve hygiene to stop complaints; add motivators for satisfaction.

sections.question: Factory workers on fixed hourly wage with strict supervision complain of boredom and high absence. Apply two motivation theories and suggest one policy change.
sections.solution:
**Taylor lens:** Output not linked to pay — **no financial incentive** for extra effort; suggest **piece rate** or **bonus for targets** — but risk quality cuts.

**Herzberg lens:** Supervision and monotonous work are **hygiene/dissatisfiers**; need **job rotation/enrichment** (motivators) — **team responsibility** for quality checks.

**Mayo lens:** Isolation on line — introduce **team huddles**, **problem-solving groups**.

**Policy:** **Team-based bonus** + **job rotation** — combines financial and human relations insights.

sections.content: Use a **two-column table**: Theory | Application to case | Limitation — saves time in 12-mark questions.
